content.text: is the intervention point pattern where the agent pauses

executing a tool call and waits for human approval. Unlike post-action review (which evaluates a completed artifact), pre-action review intercepts individual tool calls at the moment the agent would invoke them — giving a human the opportunity to inspect the tool name and its exact arguments, then approve or deny execution.

This pattern is essential for any tool with side effects that are difficult or impossible to reverse: sending emails, deleting files, executing database mutations, deploying code, or initiating financial transactions. The human acts as a runtime circuit breaker, preventing the agent from taking actions that look syntactically correct but are semantically wrong given the actual context.

attrs.alt: Pre-action review gate diagram showing agent, review gate, human approval, and risk classification tiers
attrs.title: Pre-Action Review: Gate Before Execution

content.text: Not every tool call needs human approval — that would serialize everything and eliminate the value of an agent. The decision rule is based on two axes:
(how many users/records/systems are affected if it goes wrong?). Classify each tool in your tool registry into one of four tiers:
write_file, UPDATE queries, create_ticket, internal email
Review first time per session; auto-approve repeats
delete_*, exec_shell, external email, deploy, charge_customer
drop_table, mass_email (>1 000 recipients), irreversible bulk ops
if a mistake caused by this tool call could not be explained away in a post-mortem as a recoverable configuration error, require pre-action review.
The Anthropic API returns tool calls as
array. The agent loop intercepts these before dispatching them to the actual tool. The review gate sits between the API response and the tool executor.

import anthropic, asyncio, json
from enum import StrEnum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable, Awaitable

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

class RiskTier(StrEnum):
    LOW      = "low"
    MEDIUM   = "medium"
    HIGH     = "high"
    CRITICAL = "critical"

class ReviewDecision(StrEnum):
    APPROVE = "approve"
    DENY    = "deny"

# Tool registry: map each tool name to its risk tier
TOOL_RISK: dict[str, RiskTier] = {
    "read_file":       RiskTier.LOW,
    "list_dir":        RiskTier.LOW,
    "query_db":        RiskTier.LOW,      # SELECT only
    "write_file":      RiskTier.MEDIUM,
    "update_record":   RiskTier.MEDIUM,
    "send_email":      RiskTier.HIGH,
    "delete_file":     RiskTier.HIGH,
    "exec_shell":      RiskTier.HIGH,
    "deploy_service":  RiskTier.HIGH,
    "drop_table":      RiskTier.CRITICAL,
    "mass_notify":     RiskTier.CRITICAL,
}

@dataclass
class PendingApproval:
    approval_id: str
    tool_name:   str
    tool_input:  dict
    risk_tier:   RiskTier
    event:       asyncio.Event = field(default_factory=asyncio.Event)
    decision:    ReviewDecision | None = None
    deny_reason: str = ""

# In-memory store keyed by approval_id
_pending: dict[str, PendingApproval] = {}

async def request_approval(tool_name: str, tool_input: dict) -> PendingApproval:
    import uuid
    tier = TOOL_RISK.get(tool_name, RiskTier.HIGH)   # unknown tools default to HIGH
    approval = PendingApproval(
        approval_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
        tool_name=tool_name,
        tool_input=tool_input,
        risk_tier=tier,
    )
    _pending[approval.approval_id] = approval
    return approval

def needs_review(tool_name: str, session_approved: set[str]) -> bool:
    """
    LOW  → never needs review.
    MEDIUM → only if this tool has not already been approved in this session.
    HIGH / CRITICAL → always needs review.
    """
    tier = TOOL_RISK.get(tool_name, RiskTier.HIGH)
    if tier == RiskTier.LOW:
        return False
    if tier == RiskTier.MEDIUM:
        return tool_name not in session_approved
    return True   # HIGH and CRITICAL always gate
The Agent Loop with Pre-Action Gates
block. If review is required, it creates a
s their decision. The rest of the loop continues normally regardless of whether review was required.

async def run_agent_with_review(
    task: str,
    tools: list[dict],
    tool_dispatch: dict[str, Callable[..., Awaitable[Any]]],
    notify_reviewer: Callable[[PendingApproval], Awaitable[None]],
    max_steps: int = 20,
) -> str:
    """
    Run an agent loop where high-risk tool calls are gated on human approval
    before execution. Low-risk tools execute immediately.
    """
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    session_approved: set[str] = set()   # tools approved so far this session

    for step in range(max_steps):
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=2048,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            # No more tool calls — extract final text
            return next(
                (b.text for b in response.content if hasattr(b, "text")),
                "",
            )

        # Process tool_use blocks
        tool_results = []
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type != "tool_use":
                continue

            tool_name  = block.name
            tool_input = block.input

            if needs_review(tool_name, session_approved):
                # --- Gate: pause and ask human ---
                approval = await request_approval(tool_name, tool_input)
                await notify_reviewer(approval)          # send email / push notification / etc.
                await approval.event.wait()              # block until human responds

                if approval.decision == ReviewDecision.DENY:
                    result_content = (
                        f"Tool call denied by human reviewer. "
                        f"Reason: {approval.deny_reason or 'no reason given'}. "
                        f"Do not retry this action without explicit user instruction."
                    )
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": result_content,
                        "is_error": True,
                    })
                    continue   # skip execution; the agent will handle the denial

                # Approved — remember for MEDIUM-tier auto-approval
                if TOOL_RISK.get(tool_name) == RiskTier.MEDIUM:
                    session_approved.add(tool_name)

            # --- Execute the tool ---
            fn = tool_dispatch.get(tool_name)
            if fn is None:
                result = json.dumps({"error": f"unknown tool: {tool_name}"})
            else:
                try:
                    result_obj = await fn(**tool_input)
                    result = json.dumps(result_obj) if not isinstance(result_obj, str) else result_obj
                except Exception as exc:
                    result = json.dumps({"error": str(exc)})

            tool_results.append({
                "type": "tool_result",
                "tool_use_id": block.id,
                "content": result,
            })

        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    raise RuntimeError(f"Agent exceeded max_steps={max_steps}")
The reviewer accesses a web endpoint (or CLI prompt, or Slack integration) to inspect the pending approval and submit their decision. The endpoint calls
which unblocks the waiting coroutine in
A reviewer may be unavailable. Without a timeout the agent blocks indefinitely. Use
to enforce a deadline. On timeout, default to
— the safe choice — and surface an informative message to the agent.

REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 300   # 5 minutes

async def request_and_wait(
    tool_name: str,
    tool_input: dict,
    notify: Callable[[PendingApproval], Awaitable[None]],
) -> ReviewDecision:
    """Gate a single tool call; return the reviewer's decision or deny on timeout."""
    approval = await request_approval(tool_name, tool_input)
    await notify(approval)
    try:
        await asyncio.wait_for(approval.event.wait(), timeout=REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS)
        return approval.decision or ReviewDecision.DENY
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        approval.decision  = ReviewDecision.DENY
        approval.deny_reason = f"Reviewer did not respond within {REVIEW_TIMEOUT_SECONDS}s."
        return ReviewDecision.DENY

# Integrating with the agent loop:
# Replace `await approval.event.wait()` with `await request_and_wait(...)`
# and use the returned decision directly.
tier tools, a single reviewer is insufficient. Require two independent approvals before executing. This matches the four-eyes principle used in financial services and regulated environments.
The quality of a human reviewer's decision depends on how clearly the approval request is presented. A bare JSON dump of tool arguments is often ambiguous. A well-designed approval UI includes:
: show the risk tier prominently — red for HIGH, purple for CRITICAL.
: include the task description and the last 2–3 messages so the reviewer understands
the agent chose this tool call.
: if the tool writes or updates a resource, show the before/after diff rather than the raw new content.
: time to decision should be under 30 seconds for routine approvals.

def build_approval_notification(approval: PendingApproval, task_context: str) -> dict:
    """
    Build a structured notification payload for the review UI or messaging system.
    """
    tier_labels = {
        RiskTier.LOW:      ("🟢 Low",      "Read-only operation"),
        RiskTier.MEDIUM:   ("🟡 Medium",   "Reversible write operation"),
        RiskTier.HIGH:     ("🔴 High",     "Potentially irreversible action"),
        RiskTier.CRITICAL: ("🟣 Critical", "Irreversible — two approvals required"),
    }
    label, description = tier_labels[approval.risk_tier]

    # Humanise common tool inputs
    summaries = {
        "send_email":    lambda i: f"Send email to {i.get('to')} — Subject: {i.get('subject','(no subject)')}",
        "delete_file":   lambda i: f"Permanently delete {i.get('path')}",
        "write_file":    lambda i: f"Overwrite {i.get('path')} ({len(str(i.get('content',''))) } chars)",
        "exec_shell":    lambda i: f"Run shell command: {i.get('command')}",
        "deploy_service": lambda i: f"Deploy {i.get('service')} to {i.get('environment','prod')}",
    }
    humanised = summaries.get(approval.tool_name, lambda i: json.dumps(i))(approval.tool_input)

    return {
        "approval_id":  approval.approval_id,
        "risk_tier":    label,
        "risk_note":    description,
        "action":       humanised,
        "tool_name":    approval.tool_name,
        "tool_args":    approval.tool_input,
        "task_context": task_context[:400],
        "approve_url":  f"/approvals/{approval.approval_id}/approve",
        "deny_url":     f"/approvals/{approval.approval_id}/deny",
    }

Every approval or denial is a security event. Append each decision to an immutable audit log so you can reconstruct what the agent did, who approved it, and when. This satisfies SOC 2, ISO 27001, and similar compliance requirements that mandate human oversight of automated actions.

import pathlib, datetime

AUDIT_LOG = pathlib.Path("agent_audit.jsonl")

def audit(
    approval_id: str,
    tool_name:   str,
    tool_input:  dict,
    decision:    ReviewDecision,
    reviewer:    str,
    deny_reason: str = "",
) -> None:
    entry = {
        "ts":          datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "approval_id": approval_id,
        "tool_name":   tool_name,
        "tool_input":  tool_input,
        "decision":    decision,
        "reviewer":    reviewer,
        "deny_reason": deny_reason,
    }
    with AUDIT_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

# Call audit() after every approval.event.set() in the review endpoints.
# Use an append-only PostgreSQL table in production (never UPDATE or DELETE rows).
# Query patterns:
#   SELECT * FROM audit WHERE tool_name = 'delete_file' AND decision = 'deny' ORDER BY ts DESC;
#   SELECT tool_name, COUNT(*) FROM audit WHERE decision = 'approve' GROUP BY tool_name;

: if every tool call requires review, humans stop reading carefully and auto-approve. Keep HIGH-gate calls to genuinely dangerous actions; the Low/Medium auto-approve tiers reduce alert volume substantially.
, a reviewer going offline leaves the agent blocked indefinitely. Always set a timeout and default to deny.
: the deny result must include clear instruction ("Do not retry") in the
content, or the agent will reformulate arguments and attempt the same action again.
Missing context in the approval UI
: showing only raw JSON arguments leads to poor decisions. Include the task description and recent conversation so the reviewer understands why the agent chose this action.
: auto-approving a MEDIUM tool for an entire session is fine if the tool is
— every recipient may be different. Scope auto-approval to (tool_name, key arguments) not just tool_name.

Pre-Action Review intercepts tool calls between the API response and actual execution. A review gate classifies each tool into Low (auto-approve), Medium (approve once per session), High (always review), or Critical (two-person rule) tiers. The gating logic pauses the agent coroutine on a

event, notifies the human via a web endpoint or messaging integration, and unblocks only when a decision arrives or the timeout expires. Deny decisions return a structured error result that tells the agent not to retry. Every approval decision is appended to an append-only audit log for compliance. The pattern eliminates an entire class of irreversible agent mistakes without requiring the human to approve every low-risk read operation.
